subtitle: When Clouds Remember: Kālidāsa and the Songs of an Age

panels.shot: wide, low-angle view of Kalidasa standing on a hilltop above Ujjain at dusk, monsoon clouds towering behind him
panels.action: Kalidasa stands in simple scholar’s robes on a grassy rise above ancient Ujjain, palm-leaf manuscript in hand, gazing up at massive dark monsoon clouds rolling over the city and the distant Shipra river.

dialogue.text: In the age when Gupta kings ruled and Sanskrit flowered, there walked a poet named Kālidāsa, whose words would teach even clouds to carry longing.

i18n.hi: गुप्त सम्राटों का समय था, संस्कृत खिल रही थी, और कालिदास नाम का कवि शब्दों से बादलों तक में विरह जगा देता था।

panels.shot: broad aerial view of the Gupta realm, from fertile plains to busy cities, with faint palace silhouettes
panels.action: A sweeping view shows lush fields, temple spires, and orderly towns under a calm sky, with a faint outline of a distant imperial court hinted on the horizon.

dialogue.text: The late fourth and early fifth century saw the Guptas reign over North India, a time when stone, scripture, and song were woven into a new order.

i18n.hi: चौथी-पाँचवीं सदी में गुप्तों ने उत्तर भारत संभाला, पत्थर, शास्त्र और गीत मिलकर एक नया विधान गढ़ रहे थे।

panels.shot: medium shot of learned men and artisans in a city lane of Ujjain
panels.action: In a busy street of Ujjain, merchants, stone carvers, and Brahmin teachers move past each other; a small shrine stands by the road, garlanded and worn smooth by hands.

dialogue.text: Cities like Ujjain became crossroads of trade and thought, where merchants counted coins and scholars counted meters of verse in the same morning light.

i18n.hi: उज्जयिनी जैसे नगर व्यापार और विचार के चौराहे बने, जहाँ सौदागर सिक्के गिनते और पंडित उसी भोर में छंद।

panels.shot: soft close-up of young Kalidasa seated by the Shipra riverbank
panels.action: A youthful Kalidasa sits alone on the grassy bank of the Shipra river, tracing lines in the damp soil with a reed, watching the slow current shimmer in late afternoon.

dialogue.text: Of Kālidāsa himself, the records are silent; we do not know his village, his teachers, or the names of his kin.

i18n.hi: कालिदास के बारे में खुद इतिहास चुप है; न गाँव का पता, न गुरु, न कुल का नाम।

panels.shot: medium shot from behind, showing Kalidasa looking up at temple inscriptions
panels.action: Seen from behind, Kalidasa stands in a modest temple courtyard, head tilted up to study deep-cut Sanskrit verses on stone pillars glowing in slanting sun.

dialogue.text: We know him instead through the carved echoes of others: later inscriptions naming Kālidāsa beside Vyāsa and Vālmīki, as one of the great ones of song.

i18n.hi: उसे हम औरों की गूँज से जानते हैं—शिलालेख जहाँ कालिदास का नाम व्यास, वाल्मीकि के बराबर खुदा है।

panels.shot: close-up on Kalidasa’s hand writing on a palm leaf by lamplight
panels.action: By the soft glow of an oil lamp, Kalidasa’s steady hand scratches meticulous Sanskrit lines onto a palm leaf resting on a wooden board.

dialogue.text: In that age, Sanskrit was becoming a shared high language of power and praise, and Kālidāsa learned to make it flow like a clear river through palm leaves.

i18n.hi: उस युग में संस्कृत सत्ता और स्तुति की साझी भाषा बन रही थी, और कालिदास ने उसे ताड़पत्रों पर निर्मल नदी-सा बहना सिखाया।

panels.shot: wide interior view of a royal or noble hall, with Kalidasa reciting before patrons
panels.action: In a pillared hall hung with simple banners, Kalidasa stands with composed grace, reciting to seated nobles and scholars who listen, some intent, some smiling faintly.

dialogue.text: Tradition will later place him in the glittering court of a king Vikramāditya, but the stones are quiet; they tell us only that his verses traveled through patron halls and temples.

i18n.hi: बाद की परंपरा उसे झिलमिलाते विक्रमादित्य दरबार में बैठाती है, पर पत्थर बस इतना कहते हैं कि उसके छंद राजमहलों और मंदिरों तक जा पहुँचे।

panels.shot: medium shot of Kalidasa in a forested āśrama setting, eyes closed, imagining a scene
panels.action: Kalidasa sits under a tree in a forest hermitage, eyes closed, as deer graze nearby and smoke from a distant sacrificial fire curls into the canopy.

dialogue.text: From older tales and sacred lore he gathered the threads of his art, but he wove them with a tenderness and sharpness of eye that seemed wholly his own.

i18n.hi: पुरानी कथाओं और शास्त्रों से उसने कला के धागे बटोरे, पर उन्हें ऐसी कोमलता और पैनी नज़र से बुना जो सिर्फ उसकी लगती थी।

panels.shot: dynamic wide shot of Shakuntala’s forest hermitage scene forming ghostlike around Kalidasa
panels.action: Around the seated poet, a faint, dreamlike scene takes shape: Shakuntala in bark garments watering creepers, friends laughing, a distant king glimpsed through trees, all like mist-born figures.

dialogue.text: In Abhijñānaśākuntalam, he reshaped the story of Śakuntalā, filling the forest with soft laughter, sudden desire, and the slow ache of separation.

i18n.hi: ‘अभिज्ञानशाकुंतलम’ में उसने शकुंतला की कथा बदली, वन को भर दिया हल्की हँसी, अचानक कामना और धीमे-धीमे घटते विरह से।

panels.shot: medium shot of Kalidasa performing Shakuntala for a small mixed audience, including women
panels.action: In an open courtyard lit by torches, Kalidasa gestures gently as he performs, while women and men seated together lean forward at the moment of Śakuntalā’s plea before the king.

dialogue.text: His Śakuntalā is wronged yet dignified; she speaks in a voice that both submits and resists, shaped by a world where women moved inside rules set by others.

i18n.hi: उसकी शकुंतला ठुकराई भी है, पर स्वाभिमानी भी; उसकी आवाज़ मान भी है, विरोध भी, उस दुनिया की जहाँ औरों के नियम में औरतें चलती थीं।

panels.shot: close-up on Kalidasa’s face, half-lit, showing empathy and doubt
panels.action: Kalidasa’s face is caught in half-shadow, brows slightly knit, as if weighing the sorrow he has given his heroine and the justice he can offer her in words.

dialogue.speaker: kalidasa
dialogue.text: How far may I bend the old tale, and still be true?

i18n.hi: ‘पुरानी कथा को कितना मोड़ूँ कि सच भी रहे?’

panels.shot: wide shot of a royal courtyard where a small child is crowned, echoing Raghuvamsha
panels.action: Kalidasa watches from a shaded veranda as priests crown a young prince in a courtyard, while elders and warriors stand in ordered rows around him.

dialogue.text: In Raghuvaṃśa, he turned to kings, tracing the line of Raghu and Rāma, praising rule as sacrifice and burden, each generation weighed against the last.

i18n.hi: ‘रघुवंश’ में उसने राजाओं की ओर रुख किया, रघु और राम की वंशरेखा खींची, राजधर्म को यज्ञ भी कहा, बोझ भी, हर पीढ़ी पिछली से तौली।

panels.shot: medium shot of Kalidasa at a desk, imagining Himalayan peaks and Pārvatī’s silhouette (Kumarasambhava)
panels.action: As Kalidasa writes indoors, the wall behind him seems to melt into a vision of snow peaks and the poised figure of Pārvatī in austere meditation.

dialogue.text: In Kumārasambhava, the mountains themselves rise to speak, as he tells of Pārvatī’s fierce devotion and the birth of Kumāra, born for war yet sung with delicacy.

i18n.hi: ‘कुमारसम्भव’ में तो पहाड़ ही बोल उठते हैं, जब वह पार्वती की प्रचंड तपस्या और युद्ध के लिए जन्मे, पर कोमल सुरों में गाए कुमार का जन्म कहता है।

panels.shot: low, intimate shot of Kalidasa alone on a terrace, looking at the first monsoon cloud
panels.action: Kalidasa stands alone on a flat rooftop in Ujjain, the hot season haze broken by a single dark cloud swelling on the horizon.

dialogue.text: Then, in Meghadūta, he made no king, no god the center, but a cloud, a journey, and the ache of a banished yakṣa longing for his beloved in distant Alakā.

i18n.hi: फिर ‘मेघदूत’ में उसने न देव को, न राजा को केंद्र रखा, सिर्फ बादल, एक सफ़र, और दूर अलका में प्रेयसी को तरसते निर्वासित यक्ष का मन।

panels.shot: medium shot of Kalidasa extending a hand as if addressing the sky
panels.action: He stretches one arm towards the rising cloud, eyes bright, as if speaking to a trusted messenger standing at the edge of his terrace.

dialogue.speaker: kalidasa
dialogue.text: Cloud, will you carry what a tongue cannot bear alone?

i18n.hi: बादल, क्या तू वह ढोएगा जो अकेली जीभ न ढो पाती?

panels.shot: expansive bird’s-eye view following an imagined cloud over rivers, forests, and towns
panels.action: From high above, a great monsoon cloud drifts across rivers, forests, and clustered towns, its ragged underside trailing fine rain over the land below.

dialogue.text: In his verse the cloud lingers over waterfalls, city walls, shrines and lovers’ houses, making the land itself a map of memory and desire.

i18n.hi: उसकी पंक्तियों में बादल झरनों, किलों, मंदिरों, प्रेमियों के घरों पर रुकता है, धरती को ही स्मृति और चाहत का नक्शा बना देता है।

panels.shot: tight close-up of Kalidasa’s stylus pausing over the words ‘Megha’ and ‘Dūta’ on a palm leaf
panels.action: His stylus hovers for a heartbeat above the palm leaf where neat letters spell out the opening of Meghadūta, as if he weighs the weight of this simple image.

dialogue.text: We do not know what exile of his own he wrote into that yakṣa, but the poem holds such tenderness that centuries later readers will feel their own partings inside it.

i18n.hi: हम नहीं जानते उसने किस अपना निर्वासन उस यक्ष में भर दिया, पर कविता ऐसी कोमल है कि सदियों बाद भी पाठक अपना बिछोह उसमें पहचानेंगे।

panels.shot: medium shot of an inscription carver chiseling, with Kalidasa watching from the side
panels.action: A stone mason carves careful Sanskrit lines into a pillar as Kalidasa stands quietly in the background, half in shadow, watching the letters take shape.

dialogue.text: By the seventh century, the Aihole inscription would name Kālidāsa beside the epic seers, proof that his songs had already entered the hard memory of stone.

i18n.hi: सातवीं सदी तक ऐहोल के शिलालेख में कालिदास का नाम महाकाव्य ऋषियों के साथ आया—सबूत कि उसके गीत पत्थर की कड़ी याद में उतर चुके थे।

panels.shot: wide shot bridging time, with future readers in different garb in a dim library, a faint image of Kalidasa above
panels.action: In a dim library far from India, later readers in foreign clothes sit by candlelight with a manuscript of Śakuntalā, while a faint, translucent image of Kalidasa hovers above them, serene.

dialogue.text: Many ages later, across seas, Goethe would bow before Śakuntalā, and Sanskrit drama would enter a new tongue, carrying Kālidāsa beyond the lands he knew.

i18n.hi: कई युग बाद, समंदर पार गोएथे ने शकुंतला को प्रणाम किया, और संस्कृत नाटक नई भाषा में उतरा, कालिदास को उन भूमियों से आगे ले गया जिन्हें वह जानता था।

panels.shot: low-angle shot of Kalidasa walking away along a riverbank at sunset, manuscripts under his arm
panels.action: At sunset, Kalidasa walks along the riverbank, his figure slowly receding, a small bundle of manuscripts under his arm, the sky streaked with gold and purple.

dialogue.text: Empires fade, names of kings are forgotten, but his springs, clouds, and forest paths remain, reminding us that beauty in words can keep an age breathing long after its rulers are dust.

i18n.hi: साम्राज्य ढहते हैं, राजाओं के नाम मिट जाते हैं, पर उसके झरे, बादल और वन-पथ बने रहते हैं, बताते हुए कि शब्दों की सुंदरता युगों को शासकों की राख के बाद भी साँस देती रखती है।

subtitle_i18n.en: When Clouds Remember: Kālidāsa and the Songs of an Age
subtitle_i18n.hi: जब बादल याद रखते हैं: कालिदास और एक युग के गीत
